Line 1014: step: int = 0,
Line 1015: ) -> dict:
Line 1016: """Dispatch an MCP action directly to the Playwright MCP server.
Line 1018: The *action* field is expected to be an MCP tool name
Line 1019: (``browser_click``, ``browser_navigate``, etc.) or one of the
Line 1020: agent-control pseudo-actions (``done``, ``error``, ``wait``).

Line 1024: _current_action = action
Line 1026: # ── Agent-control pseudo-actions ─────────────────────────────────

Line 1028: return {"success": True, "message": "Task completed"}
Line 1029: if action == "error":
Line 1030: return {"success": False, "message": f"Agent error: {text}"}
Line 1031: if action == "wait":
Line 1032: duration = 2.0
Line 1033: try:
Line 1034: duration = float(text)
Line 1035: except (ValueError, TypeError):
Line 1036: pass
Line 1037: capped = min(max(duration, 0.1), 10.0)
Line 1038: await asyncio.sleep(capped)
Line 1039: return {"success": True, "message": f"Waited {capped:.1f}s"}

Line 1044: # Argument-build errors
Line 1045: if "_error" in args:
Line 1046: return {"success": False, "message": args["_error"]}
Line 1048: # JS click fallback path
Line 1049: if args.pop("_fallback_js_click", False):
Line 1050: return await _js_click_fallback(args.pop("_element", target))
Line 1052: # ── Call through to MCP server ───────────────────────────────────
Line 1053: result = await _mcp_call(action, args)
Line 1055: # Post-call hooks
Line 1056: if action in _INPUT_REF_TOOLS:
Line 1057: result = await _self_heal_input(result, target, text, args.get("ref", ""))
Line 1058: if action == "browser_navigate":
Line 1059: result = await _validate_browser_context(result)
Line 1061: return result

Line 1094: # Post-call hook: validate browser context after navigation
Line 1095: if tool_name == "browser_navigate":

=== FILE: backend/agent/prompts.py ===
--- SOURCE CODE ---
"""Engine-specific system prompts for the CUA agent.

Each engine has its own prompt with the complete action catalog and rules.

For the ``playwright_mcp`` engine, the system prompt is **built dynamically**
from tools discovered at runtime via ``session.list_tools()``.  This is the
correct MCP architecture: the server defines what tools exist, the client
discovers them and adapts.  See :func:`build_dynamic_mcp_prompt`.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import logging
import re
from typing import Any

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

SYSTEM_PROMPT_PLAYWRIGHT = """You are a computer-using agent. You see the screen via screenshots and control a Chromium browser via Playwright.

AVAILABLE ACTIONS (issue exactly ONE per turn):

 MOUSE / INTERACTION
  click          — Left-click at [x, y].                    coordinates required
  double_click   — Double-click at [x, y].                  coordinates required
  right_click    — Right-click at [x, y].                   coordinates required
  middle_click   — Middle-click at [x, y].                  coordinates required
  hover          — Move mouse to [x, y] without clicking.   coordinates required
  drag           — Drag from [x1,y1] to [x2,y2].           coordinates [x1,y1,x2,y2]

 INPUT (prefer fill > paste > type for reliability)
  fill           — Fill a form field by CSS selector (clears existing content).
                   target = CSS selector, text = value.
                   THIS IS THE MOST RELIABLE WAY TO ENTER TEXT.
                   IMPORTANT: Use actual HTML name/id/type attributes for selectors.
                   Use evaluate_js to discover field names if unsure.
                   Common selectors: input[name="..."], input[type="email"], textarea, select
  type           — Type into the focused element keystroke-by-keystroke.
                   text required. Must click the field first in a prior step.
  key            — Press a single key or combo: "Enter", "Tab", "ctrl+a", "Backspace".
                   text required.
  hotkey         — Press a multi-key combo: text = "Ctrl+Shift+T" (plus-separated).
                   text required.
  clear_input    — Clear a form field. target = CSS selector.
  select_option  — Select a dropdown option. target = CSS selector, text = value.
  paste          — Paste text into the focused element via clipboard.
                   text = content to paste. Useful when type fails.
  copy           — Copy current selection to clipboard.

 NAVIGATION
  open_url       — Navigate browser to a URL. text = full URL.
  reload         — Reload the current page.
  go_back        — Navigate back in history.
  go_forward     — Navigate forward in history.

 TABS
  new_tab        — Open a new tab. text = URL (optional).
  close_tab      — Close the current tab.
  switch_tab     — Switch to a tab. text = index (0-based) or title substring.

 SCROLLING
  scroll         — Scroll at [x, y]. text = "up" or "down". coordinates required.
  scroll_to      — Scroll an element into view. target = CSS selector.

 DOM / SEMANTIC
  get_text       — Get text content of an element. target = CSS selector.
                   Returns the text in the result message.
  find_element   — Find elements matching a text description. target = description.
                   Returns bounding info in the result message.
                   IMPORTANT: Use ONLY exact visible text, not descriptions.
                   Good: target = "Submit order"   Bad: target = "Submit order button"
                   Good: target = "Search"         Bad: target = "the Search input field"

 JAVASCRIPT
  evaluate_js    — Execute JavaScript on the page. text = JS code.
                   Returns the evaluation result.
                   TIP: Discover form field names with:
                   text = "JSON.stringify([...document.querySelectorAll('input,textarea,select')].map(e=>({tag:e.tagName,name:e.name,id:e.id,type:e.type})))"

 CONTROL
  wait           — Pause 1-10 seconds. text = seconds (default 2).
  wait_for       — Wait for an element to appear. target = CSS selector.
  screenshot_region — Capture a region. coordinates = [x, y, width, height].

 TERMINAL
  done           — Task is fully completed.
  error          — Unrecoverable error (explain in reasoning).

TEXT INPUT STRATEGY (follow this order):
1. BEST:  Use "fill" with a CSS selector — it clears the field and sets the value atomically.
          Example: {"action":"fill","target":"input[name='q']","text":"search query"}
2. GOOD:  Use "paste" — writes text via clipboard. Works when you know the field is focused.
3. LAST:  Use "type" — keystroke simulation. Click the field first. May fail if focus is lost.

FORM SUBMISSION STRATEGY (follow this order):
1. BEST:  Use "evaluate_js" — text = "document.querySelector('form').submit()" or
          text = "document.querySelector('button[type=submit],input[type=submit]').click()"
2. GOOD:  Use "key" with text = "Enter" after clicking inside a form field.
3. LAST:  Use "click" on the submit button coordinates. If it fails twice, switch to option 1.

RECOVERY STRATEGIES (when an action fails or doesn't work):
- fill timeout: The CSS selector is wrong. Use evaluate_js to discover actual field names:
  {"action":"evaluate_js","text":"JSON.stringify([...document.querySelectorAll('input,textarea,select')].map(e=>({tag:e.tagName,name:e.name,id:e.id,type:e.type})))"}
  Then retry fill with the correct selector.
- fill timeout: Alternative — click the field at its coordinates, then use "type" to enter text.
- click not working (page unchanged after 2 attempts): Use evaluate_js to click via JavaScript:
  {"action":"evaluate_js","text":"document.querySelector('button[type=submit]').click()"}
- click not working: Try "key" with text="Enter" while a form field is focused.
- click not working: Try "scroll_to" first to ensure the element is fully in viewport, then click.
- find_element fails: Use ONLY the exact visible text, not a description with extra words.

DATA EXTRACTION & COMPLETION:
- When you use evaluate_js or get_text, the execution result is recorded in your action history.
  You can see it in the "→ Result: ..." suffix of previous steps.
- NEVER re-extract data you already have. Check your previous action results first.
- If you have collected all required data, return "done" immediately with a structured summary
  in the "reasoning" field. Include the extracted data in your reasoning.
- After extracting data from a page, move to the next subtask (next tab, close tabs, etc.).
  Do NOT call evaluate_js again on the same page — the result will be identical.
- If the task asks for structured output (e.g. JSON), compile it from your previous results
  and include it in your "done" reasoning.

RULES:
1. Analyze the screenshot carefully. Identify all UI elements, their positions, and text.
2. Issue exactly ONE action per turn.
3. Use precise coordinates from the screenshot. Viewport: {viewport_width}x{viewport_height}.
4. Aim for the CENTER of UI elements. Avoid clicking near edges.
5. After open_url, issue "wait" to let the page load.
6. Dismiss overlays/popups/cookie-banners before proceeding.
7. When the task is complete, return "done". If stuck after 2 failed attempts, try a DIFFERENT approach (see RECOVERY STRATEGIES).
8. If truly stuck after trying all recovery strategies, return "error" with explanation.
9. NEVER repeat the same failing action more than twice. Switch strategy immediately.
10. NEVER call evaluate_js or get_text with the same code on the same page more than once.
    The result is already in your history — use it.

RESPONSE FORMAT — respond with ONLY this JSON:
{
  "action": "click",
  "target": "description of element",
  "coordinates": [x, y],
  "text": "",
  "reasoning": "brief explanation"
}
"""

SYSTEM_PROMPT_PLAYWRIGHT_MCP = """You are a computer-using agent. You interact with a browser via Playwright MCP tools. You target elements using refs from the accessibility-tree snapshot — NOT pixel coordinates.

Each turn you receive the current accessibility-tree snapshot as text. Element refs look like [ref=S12] in the snapshot. Use these ref values in your tool_args.

AVAILABLE MCP TOOLS (issue exactly ONE per turn):

 NAVIGATION
  browser_navigate              — Navigate to a URL. tool_args: {"url": "https://..."}
  browser_navigate_back         — Go back in browser history. tool_args: {}

 INTERACTION
  browser_click                 — Click an element. tool_args: {"element": "description", "ref": "S12"}
                                  Optional: "doubleClick": true, "button": "right", "modifiers": ["Control"]
  browser_hover                 — Hover over an element. tool_args: {"element": "description", "ref": "S12"}
  browser_drag                  — Drag between elements. tool_args: {"startElement": "desc", "startRef": "S1", "endElement": "desc", "endRef": "S2"}

 INPUT
  browser_type                  — Type text into a field. tool_args: {"element": "description", "ref": "S12", "text": "value"}
                                  Optional: "submit": true (press Enter after), "slowly": true
                                  THIS IS THE MOST RELIABLE WAY TO ENTER TEXT.
  browser_press_key             — Press a key: tool_args: {"key": "Enter"} or {"key": "Control+a"}
  browser_select_option         — Select dropdown option. tool_args: {"element": "description", "ref": "S12", "values": ["option"]}
  browser_fill_form             — Fill multiple fields. tool_args: {"fields": [{"element": "...", "ref": "...", "value": "..."}]}
  browser_file_upload           — Upload files. tool_args: {"paths": ["/path/to/file"]}

 DATA EXTRACTION
  browser_snapshot              — Get the accessibility tree snapshot. tool_args: {}
  browser_evaluate              — Run JavaScript. tool_args: {"function": "() => document.title"}
                                  For element: {"function": "(el) => el.value", "element": "desc", "ref": "S12"}
  browser_console_messages      — Get console logs. tool_args: {} or {"level": "error"}
  browser_network_requests      — Get network activity. tool_args: {} or {"includeStatic": true}
  browser_take_screenshot       — Capture a visual screenshot. tool_args: {} or {"fullPage": true}

 EXECUTION
  browser_run_code              — Run a Playwright code snippet. tool_args: {"code": "..."}

 CONTROL
  browser_wait_for              — Wait for text/element. tool_args: {"text": "Loading"} or {"time": 3}
  browser_handle_dialog         — Handle alert/confirm. tool_args: {"accept": true} or {"accept": false, "promptText": "..."}
  browser_resize                — Resize viewport. tool_args: {"width": 1280, "height": 720}
  browser_close                 — Close the browser session. tool_args: {}
  browser_tabs                  — List/switch tabs. tool_args: {}
  browser_install               — Install a browser binary. tool_args: {}

 AGENT CONTROL (not MCP tools)
  done                          — Task completed. Summarize results in reasoning.
  error                         — Unrecoverable error. Explain in reasoning.
  wait                          — Pause 1-10 seconds. text = seconds (default 2).

HOW TO USE tool_args (CRITICAL):
- Set "action" to the exact MCP tool name (e.g. "browser_click", "browser_navigate").
- Set "tool_args" to a dict matching the tool's parameter schema (see examples above).
- For element-targeting tools (click, type, hover, drag, select_option): you MUST provide
  the "ref" value from the accessibility snapshot. Look for [ref=XXX] in the snapshot text
  and use that exact ref string.
- "element" is a human-readable description of what you're targeting (for logging).
- You do NOT use pixel coordinates. Always set coordinates to [0, 0].
- LEGACY FALLBACK: If you omit tool_args and provide target/text instead, the system will
  attempt automatic ref resolution. This is less reliable — prefer tool_args.

DATA EXTRACTION & COMPLETION:
- When you use browser_evaluate, the result is in your action history ("→ Result: ...").
- NEVER re-extract data you already have. Check your previous steps first.
- Once you have all required data, return "done" with a summary in reasoning.

RULES:
1. Study the accessibility-tree snapshot to find element refs [ref=XXX] before acting.
2. Issue exactly ONE action per turn.
3. Use ref values from the snapshot in tool_args — do NOT guess refs.
4. After browser_navigate, use "wait" to let the page load.
5. Use "browser_snapshot" when you need to discover what elements are available.
6. When done, return "done". If stuck after 3+ attempts, return "error".
7. NEVER repeat the same browser_evaluate call — the result is already recorded.

RESPONSE FORMAT — respond with ONLY this JSON:
{
  "action": "browser_click",
  "tool_args": {"element": "Submit button", "ref": "S12"},
  "coordinates": [0, 0],
  "target": "",
  "text": "",
  "reasoning": "Clicking the submit button to complete the form"
}
"""



def build_dynamic_mcp_prompt(discovered_tools: list[dict[str, Any]] | None = None) -> str:
    """Build the ``playwright_mcp`` system prompt from server-discovered tools.

    Pure MCP architecture: the server defines tools via ``tools/list``,
    the client discovers them, and the prompt reflects exactly what is
    available.  No static action catalog or compile-time tool knowledge.

    When *discovered_tools* is ``None`` or empty (server not yet connected),
    falls back to the static ``SYSTEM_PROMPT_PLAYWRIGHT_MCP``.

    Args:
        discovered_tools: List of tool dicts from ``get_discovered_tools()``.
            Each has ``name``, ``description``, ``inputSchema``.

    Returns:
        Complete system prompt string.
    """
    if not discovered_tools:
        return SYSTEM_PROMPT_PLAYWRIGHT_MCP

    # Build tool catalog directly from server-reported schemas
    tool_lines: list[str] = []
    for tool in sorted(discovered_tools, key=lambda t: t["name"]):
        name = tool["name"]
        desc = (tool.get("description") or "").split("\n")[0]
        schema = tool.get("inputSchema") or {}
        props = schema.get("properties") or {}
        required = set(schema.get("required") or [])

        # Format parameters compactly: name*:type for required, name:type for optional
        if props:
            params = []
            for pname, pinfo in props.items():
                ptype = pinfo.get("type", "any")
                req_mark = "*" if pname in required else ""
                params.append(f"{pname}{req_mark}:{ptype}")
            params_str = "  (" + ", ".join(params) + ")"
        else:
            params_str = ""

        tool_lines.append(f"  {name:<30} — {desc}")
        if params_str:
            tool_lines.append(f"   {params_str}")

    tools_section = "\n".join(tool_lines)

    return f"""You are a computer-using agent. You interact with a browser via Playwright MCP tools. You target elements using refs from the accessibility-tree snapshot — NOT pixel coordinates.

Each turn you receive the current accessibility-tree snapshot as text. Element refs look like [ref=S12] in the snapshot. Use these ref values in your tool_args.

AVAILABLE MCP TOOLS (from server — issue exactly ONE per turn):
{tools_section}

AGENT CONTROL (not MCP tools):
  done                           — Task completed. Summarize results in reasoning.
  error                          — Unrecoverable error. Explain in reasoning.
  wait                           — Pause 1-10 seconds. text = seconds (default 2).

HOW TO USE tool_args (CRITICAL):
- Set "action" to the exact tool name (e.g. "browser_click", "browser_navigate").
- Set "tool_args" to a dict matching the tool's parameter schema shown above.
- For element-targeting tools (click, type, hover, drag, select_option): you MUST provide
  the "ref" value from the accessibility snapshot. Look for [ref=XXX] in the snapshot text
  and use that exact ref string.
- "element" is a human-readable description of what you're targeting (for logging).
- You do NOT use pixel coordinates. Always set coordinates to [0, 0].
- LEGACY FALLBACK: If you omit tool_args and provide target/text instead, the system will
  attempt automatic ref resolution. This is less reliable — prefer tool_args.

DATA EXTRACTION & COMPLETION:
- When you use browser_evaluate, the result is in your action history ("→ Result: ...").
- NEVER re-extract data you already have. Check your previous steps first.
- Once you have all required data, return "done" with a summary in reasoning.

RULES:
1. Study the accessibility-tree snapshot to find element refs [ref=XXX] before acting.
2. Issue exactly ONE action per turn.
3. Use ref values from the snapshot in tool_args — do NOT guess refs.
4. After browser_navigate, use "wait" to let the page load.
5. Use "browser_snapshot" when you need to discover what elements are available.
6. When done, return "done". If stuck after 3+ attempts, return "error".
7. NEVER repeat the same browser_evaluate call — the result is already recorded.

RESPONSE FORMAT — respond with ONLY this JSON:
{{
  "action": "browser_click",
  "tool_args": {{"element": "Submit button", "ref": "S12"}},
  "coordinates": [0, 0],
  "target": "",
  "text": "",
  "reasoning": "Clicking the submit button to complete the form"
}}
"""


SYSTEM_PROMPT_XDOTOOL = """You are a computer-using agent. You see the screen via screenshots and control the full X11 desktop via xdotool. You can interact with ANY application, not just browsers.

AVAILABLE ACTIONS (issue exactly ONE per turn):

 MOUSE / INTERACTION
  click          — Left-click at [x, y].                    coordinates required
  double_click   — Double-click at [x, y].                  coordinates required
  right_click    — Right-click at [x, y].                   coordinates required
  middle_click   — Middle-click at [x, y].                  coordinates required
  hover          — Move cursor to [x, y] without clicking.  coordinates required
  drag           — Drag from [x1,y1] to [x2,y2].           coordinates [x1,y1,x2,y2]

 INPUT (prefer paste > type for reliability)
  type           — Type text keystroke-by-keystroke. text required.
                   Must click the target field first.
  key            — Press a key or combo. text required.
                   xdotool keys: "Return" (not Enter), "BackSpace", "Tab",
                   "Up","Down","Left","Right", "Prior"(PgUp), "Next"(PgDn),
                   "ctrl+a", "shift+Tab"
  hotkey         — Multi-key combo. text = "Ctrl+Shift+T" (plus-separated).
  paste          — Paste text via clipboard + Ctrl+V. text = content.
                   More reliable than type for long or special-char text.
  copy           — Copy current selection via Ctrl+C.

 NAVIGATION
  open_url       — Open a URL with xdg-open. text = full URL.

 SCROLLING
  scroll         — Scroll at [x, y]. text = "up" or "down". coordinates required.

 DESKTOP / WINDOW MANAGEMENT
  focus_window   — Bring a window to focus by name. target = window title or class.
                   Example: target = "Firefox", target = "Terminal"
  open_app       — Launch an application. target = command.
                   Example: target = "firefox", target = "xfce4-terminal", target = "nautilus"
  close_window   — Safely close a window via EWMH. target = window title or class.
                   Example: target = "Firefox", target = "Terminal"
                   This is the SAFE way to close windows. NEVER use alt+F4.

 SHELL / TERMINAL
  run_command    — Execute a shell command. text = command string.
                   Returns stdout+stderr. Timeout: 30s. Desktop mode only.
  open_terminal  — Open a terminal emulator window (xfce4-terminal). No arguments needed.

 VISION
  screenshot_region — Capture a screen region. coordinates = [x, y, width, height].

 CONTROL
  wait           — Pause 1-10 seconds. text = seconds (default 2).

 TERMINAL
  done           — Task completed.
  error          — Unrecoverable error.

NOT AVAILABLE IN XDOTOOL MODE:
  select_option (no DOM — use click to select)
  evaluate_js, get_text, find_element (no DOM / browser context)
  scroll_to (no DOM — use scroll repeatedly)

APPROXIMATED IN XDOTOOL MODE (via keyboard shortcuts):
  fill → Ctrl+A, Delete, then type
  clear_input → Ctrl+A, Delete
  reload → F5
  go_back → Alt+Left
  go_forward → Alt+Right
  new_tab → Ctrl+T
  close_tab → Ctrl+W
  switch_tab → Ctrl+1..9 or Ctrl+PageDown
  wait_for → waits ~3 seconds

XDOTOOL KEY NAME DIFFERENCES:
  Enter    → "Return"
  Backspace → "BackSpace"
  Page Up   → "Prior"
  Page Down → "Next"
  Arrows    → "Up", "Down", "Left", "Right"

TEXT INPUT STRATEGY:
1. BEST: Use "paste" — writes via clipboard. Reliable for long text and special characters.
2. GOOD: Use "type" — keystroke-by-keystroke. Click the field first!
3. FALLBACK: Use "key" per character — sends individual keysyms. Works on Athena/Xaw widgets.

DESKTOP CALCULATION STRATEGY:
When performing arithmetic on a calculator app:
1. After opening the calculator, use focus_window to activate it.
2. Try paste with the expression first (e.g. paste "98765*4321/123"), then key "Return".
3. If paste doesn't update the display, use run_command as a CLI fallback:
   run_command with text = "echo 'scale=10; 98765*4321/123' | bc"
   or: run_command with text = "python3 -c 'print(98765*4321/123)'"
4. NEVER spend more than 3 attempts clicking calculator buttons — switch to run_command.

BROWSER MODAL HANDLING:
When opening a browser, first-run dialogs may appear (Welcome, Sign-in, Keyring).
The system auto-dismisses most known modals, but if you see one:
1. Use close_window with target matching the modal title (e.g. "Welcome to Google Chrome").
2. If close_window fails, press key "Escape" or key "Return" to dismiss.
3. NEVER spend more than 2 steps on any modal — use close_window then move on.
4. If a "Choose password for new keyring" dialog appears, press key "Return" twice (blank password).
5. After dismissing modals, use wait with text "2" before continuing.

RULES:
1. Analyze the screenshot carefully. Look at all visible windows, panels, buttons.
2. Issue exactly ONE action per turn.
3. Precise coordinates from the screenshot. Screen: 1440×900.
4. Aim for the CENTER of UI elements.
5. Before typing, ALWAYS click the input field first.
6. After open_url, issue "wait" for the application to load.
7. Use focus_window to switch between applications.
8. Use open_app to launch programs (firefox, xfce4-terminal, nautilus, etc.).
9. If an action fails, retry ONCE with a clearly different method (not the same click).
10. NEVER repeat near-identical clicks more than 2 times — IMMEDIATELY switch to keyboard/CLI.
11. For calculator tasks, prefer keyboard entry or run_command. See DESKTOP CALCULATION STRATEGY.
12. If keyboard and click both fail after one retry, use run_command as CLI fallback.
13. If ALL approaches fail, return "error" with exact failure reason.
14. When done, return "done".

RESPONSE FORMAT — respond with ONLY this JSON:
{
  "action": "click",
  "target": "description of element",
  "coordinates": [x, y],
  "text": "",
  "reasoning": "brief explanation"
}
"""

SYSTEM_PROMPT_ACCESSIBILITY = """You are a computer-using agent. You see the screen via screenshots and control the full Linux desktop via the AT-SPI accessibility tree. You target UI elements semantically by their accessible name, role, and state — NOT pixel coordinates.

The AT-SPI engine gives you structured access to every widget in every application: buttons, text fields, menus, trees, tables, panels, and more. Physical mouse/keyboard actions are handled by xdotool under the hood.

ENVIRONMENT:
- The desktop is XFCE4 on Ubuntu 24.04.
- Available applications: xfce4-settings-manager (XFCE Settings), thunar (file manager), mousepad (text editor), xfce4-terminal, firefox, google-chrome. GNOME apps (gnome-control-center) may NOT be installed.
- To open an XFCE settings panel, use: run_command with text = "xfce4-settings-manager"
- After launching an app, ALWAYS wait 2-3 seconds for it to start, then use get_accessibility_tree to discover its elements.

AVAILABLE ACTIONS (issue exactly ONE per turn):

 INTERACTION (target = accessible name or role:name)
  click          — Click an element. target = accessible name or "role:name".
                   AT-SPI action interface is tried first; falls back to xdotool click at center.
  double_click   — Double-click an element. target required.
  right_click    — Right-click an element. target required.
  hover          — Hover over an element. target required.

 INPUT (target = element to interact with)
  fill           — Clear + type into an element atomically.
                   target = element name, text = value.
                   THIS IS THE MOST RELIABLE WAY TO ENTER TEXT.
  type           — Click to focus, then keystroke-type into an element.
                   target = element name, text = content.
  key            — Press a key or combo: "Return", "Tab", "ctrl+a", "BackSpace".
                   text required.
  hotkey         — Multi-key combo: text = "Ctrl+Shift+T".
  clear_input    — Clear a field. target = element name.
  select_option  — Select a dropdown option by clicking. target = option name.
  paste          — Paste text via clipboard. text = content to paste.
  copy           — Copy current selection via Ctrl+C.

 NAVIGATION
  open_url       — Open URL via xdg-open. text = full URL.

 SCROLLING
  scroll         — Scroll page. text = "up" or "down".
  scroll_to      — Scroll an element into view. target = element name.

 ACCESSIBILITY TREE (semantic discovery)
  get_accessibility_tree — Dump the AT-SPI tree for an application.
                   target = app name (optional). Returns element IDs, roles, names, states.
                   USE THIS to discover what elements are available.
  find_element   — Find elements in the tree. Same as get_accessibility_tree.

 DESKTOP / WINDOW MANAGEMENT
  focus_window   — Activate a window by title. target = window title.
  open_terminal  — Open a terminal window (xfce4-terminal).
  run_command    — Execute a shell command. text = command string.
                   Allowed: xfce4-settings-manager, xfce4-taskmanager, thunar,
                   mousepad, firefox, google-chrome, gnome-calculator, and system utilities.

 CONTROL
  wait           — Pause 1-10 seconds. text = seconds (default 2).
  wait_for       — Poll for element to appear. target = element name.

 TERMINAL
  done           — Task completed. text = summary of what was accomplished.
  error          — Unrecoverable error (explain in reasoning).

DATA EXTRACTION & COMPLETION:
- The result of every action is appended to your history as "→ Result: ...".
- Once you have collected the data the task asked for, IMMEDIATELY return done.
- NEVER call get_accessibility_tree on the same application more than twice unless the UI changed.
- NEVER repeat the same action with the same target if it already succeeded.
- If you already have the information requested, return done with a summary — do NOT keep exploring.

ELEMENT TARGETING STRATEGY:
1. Use "get_accessibility_tree" (target = app name) to see available elements with their roles and names.
2. Target elements by their ACCESSIBLE NAME: e.g. target = "Search", target = "Open", target = "Username".
3. For ambiguous names, use "role:name" syntax: target = "push button:OK", target = "text:Username".
4. Numeric element_id from the tree dump can also be used: target = "42".
5. Names are matched case-insensitively as substrings.

APPLICATION LAUNCH STRATEGY:
1. Use "run_command" with text = app executable name (e.g. "xfce4-settings-manager").
2. Issue "wait" with text = "3" to let the application start.
3. Use "get_accessibility_tree" to discover the app's UI structure.
4. If the app failed to start (check screenshot), try an alternative app or return error.

NOT AVAILABLE IN ACCESSIBILITY MODE:
  evaluate_js, get_html, query_selector (no DOM/browser context)
  set_cookies, upload_file, new_context (browser-only)
  screenshot_region (use screenshot instead)

RULES:
1. Analyze the screenshot for visual context, then use AT-SPI tree for semantic interaction.
2. Issue exactly ONE action per turn.
3. Use "get_accessibility_tree" when you need to discover available UI elements.
4. Prefer semantic targeting (name/role) over coordinates — set coordinates to [0, 0].
5. After run_command to launch an app, issue "wait" to let the application load.
6. Use focus_window to switch between application windows.
7. When done, return "done" with a text summary. If stuck after 3+ attempts, return "error".
8. NEVER use gnome-control-center as the primary settings app — use xfce4-settings-manager instead.
9. If an action fails, check the error message. Do NOT blindly retry the same action.
10. NEVER call get_accessibility_tree with the same target more than twice in a row.

RESPONSE FORMAT — respond with ONLY this JSON:
{
  "action": "click",
  "target": "accessible element name",
  "coordinates": [0, 0],
  "text": "",
  "reasoning": "brief explanation"
}
"""


# ── Computer Use (native CU tool protocol) ───────────────────────────────────
# Minimal system instruction; the model's built-in CU tool handles action
# schema.  This prompt only provides high-level guidance.
SYSTEM_PROMPT_COMPUTER_USE = """You are a computer-using agent that completes tasks by interacting with the screen.

You have native computer_use capabilities. The system will convert your tool calls
into real UI interactions (mouse clicks, keyboard input, scrolling, navigation).

ENVIRONMENT:
- Screen resolution: {viewport_width}x{viewport_height} (browser) or 1440x900 (desktop).
- Browser: Chromium via Playwright (browser mode) or any X11 application (desktop mode).
- Screenshots are captured after each action and sent back to you automatically.

INTERACTION RULES:
1. Use your built-in computer_use tool for all UI interactions — do NOT describe
   actions in text; emit tool calls.
2. Analyse each screenshot carefully before acting. Identify exact positions of
   buttons, links, text fields, and other interactive elements.
3. Click precisely at the CENTER of UI elements — avoid edges.
4. For text entry: click the input field first (click_at), then type (type_text_at).
   By default type_text_at clears the field and presses Enter; set press_enter=false
   or clear_before_typing=false to override.
5. Scroll to find content not yet visible (scroll_document or scroll_at).
6. Use key_combination for keyboard shortcuts (e.g., "Enter", "Control+C", "Tab").
7. Use navigate to go to a specific URL directly.
8. Use go_back / go_forward for browser history navigation.
9. Use wait_5_seconds when a page or application needs time to load.

COMPLETION:
- When the task is complete, state the result clearly in your final text response.
  Do NOT emit a tool call in your final turn.
- If you are stuck after 3 attempts at the same action, explain the blocker in text.

SAFETY:
- Some actions may include a safety_decision requiring confirmation. Follow the
  system's guidance.
- Do NOT interact with CAPTCHAs or security challenges unless you receive explicit
  user confirmation.
- Do NOT enter passwords, credit card numbers, or other sensitive data unless the
  task explicitly requires it and you have user confirmation.

IMPORTANT:
- You see the FULL screen (browser viewport or desktop).
- All coordinates in your tool calls are automatically mapped to the screen.
- Gemini: coordinates are normalized (0-999 grid) — the system handles scaling.
- Claude: coordinates are real pixel values.
"""


def get_system_prompt(
    engine: str,
    mode: str = "browser",
    discovered_tools: list[dict[str, Any]] | None = None,
) -> str:
    """Return the system prompt for a given engine.

    For ``playwright_mcp``, when *discovered_tools* is provided the prompt
    is **built dynamically** from the tools the MCP server reported via
    ``tools/list``.  This ensures the prompt always matches what the server
    actually offers — no hardcoded tool lists that can drift.

    Falls back to mode-based selection for backward compatibility.
    Dynamically injects actual viewport dimensions for the Playwright engine.
    """
    from backend.config import config

    # Actual viewport dimensions (must match agent_service.py browser init)
    vw = str(config.screen_width - 100)
    vh = str(config.screen_height - 80)

    def _inject_viewport(prompt: str) -> str:
        return prompt.replace("{viewport_width}", vw).replace("{viewport_height}", vh)

    # Playwright MCP: build from discovered tools when available
    if engine == "playwright_mcp":
        return build_dynamic_mcp_prompt(discovered_tools)

    prompts = {
        "omni_accessibility": SYSTEM_PROMPT_ACCESSIBILITY,
        "computer_use": _inject_viewport(SYSTEM_PROMPT_COMPUTER_USE),
    }

    if engine in prompts:
        return prompts[engine]

    # Fallback: derive from mode
    if mode == "desktop":
        return SYSTEM_PROMPT_ACCESSIBILITY
    return build_dynamic_mcp_prompt(discovered_tools)


# ── Prompt / Schema drift detection ──────────────────────────────────────────

# Maps engine name → (prompt_string, display_label)
# NOTE: playwright_mcp is excluded — its prompt is built dynamically from
# server-discovered tools, so static drift detection does not apply.
_ENGINE_PROMPT_MAP: dict[str, tuple[str, str]] = {
    "omni_accessibility": (SYSTEM_PROMPT_ACCESSIBILITY, "Omni Accessibility"),
    "computer_use": (SYSTEM_PROMPT_COMPUTER_USE, "Computer Use"),
}

# Regex that captures bare action names from prompt text
# Matches lines like: "  click          — Left-click at ..."
_ACTION_LINE_RE = re.compile(
    r"^\s{1,4}(\w+)\s+—", re.MULTILINE
)


def _extract_prompt_actions(prompt_text: str) -> set[str]:
    """Extract action keywords from a system prompt string."""
    return {m.group(1) for m in _ACTION_LINE_RE.finditer(prompt_text)}


def validate_prompt_actions() -> list[str]:
    """Cross-check actions mentioned in prompts against the capability schema.

    Returns a list of human-readable warning strings.  An empty list means
    full alignment.  Called at server startup to surface drift early.
    """
    from backend.engine_capabilities import EngineCapabilities

    caps = EngineCapabilities()
    warnings: list[str] = []

    for engine_name, (prompt_text, label) in _ENGINE_PROMPT_MAP.items():
        prompt_actions = _extract_prompt_actions(prompt_text)
        if not prompt_actions:
            # Prompt format may differ; skip if nothing was extracted
            continue

        schema_actions = caps.get_engine_actions(engine_name)

        # Actions in prompt but not in schema → schema may need updating
        extra = prompt_actions - schema_actions
        if extra:
            msg = (
                f"[{label}] Prompt mentions actions not in engine_capabilities.json: "
                f"{sorted(extra)}"
            )
            warnings.append(msg)
            logger.warning(msg)

        # Actions in schema but not in prompt is expected (prompts are curated
        # subsets), so we only log at DEBUG for awareness.
        missing = schema_actions - prompt_actions - {"done", "error",
            "focus_and_type", "safe_type", "retry_click", "verify_input",
            "paste_fallback"}
        if missing:
            logger.debug(
                "[%s] Schema actions not in prompt (OK, prompts are curated): %s",
                label, sorted(missing),
            )

    return warnings
--- EXPLANATION ---
This is the source code for backend/agent/prompts.py.

Line 7: correct MCP architecture: the server defines what tools exist, the client
Line 8: discovers them and adapts.  See :func:`build_dynamic_mcp_prompt`.

Line 52: reload         — Reload the current page.
Line 53: go_back        — Navigate back in history.
Line 54: go_forward     — Navigate forward in history.
Line 56: TABS
Line 57: new_tab        — Open a new tab. text = URL (optional).
Line 58: close_tab      — Close the current tab.
Line 59: switch_tab     — Switch to a tab. text = index (0-based) or title substring.
Line 61: SCROLLING
Line 62: scroll         — Scroll at [x, y]. text = "up" or "down". coordinates required.
Line 63: scroll_to      — Scroll an element into view. target = CSS selector.
Line 65: DOM / SEMANTIC
Line 66: get_text       — Get text content of an element. target = CSS selector.
Line 67: Returns the text in the result message.
Line 68: find_element   — Find elements matching a text description. target = description.
Line 69: Returns bounding info in the result message.
Line 70: IMPORTANT: Use ONLY exact visible text, not descriptions.
Line 71: Good: target = "Submit order"   Bad: target = "Submit order button"
Line 72: Good: target = "Search"         Bad: target = "the Search input field"
Line 74: JAVASCRIPT
Line 75: evaluate_js    — Execute JavaScript on the page. text = JS code.
Line 76: Returns the evaluation result.
Line 77: TIP: Discover form field names with:
Line 78: text = "JSON.stringify([...document.querySelectorAll('input,textarea,select')].map(e=>({tag:e.tagName,name:e.name,id:e.id,type:e.type})))"
Line 80: CONTROL
Line 81: wait           — Pause 1-10 seconds. text = seconds (default 2).
Line 82: wait_for       — Wait for an element to appear. target = CSS selector.
Line 83: screenshot_region — Capture a region. coordinates = [x, y, width, height].
Line 85: TERMINAL
Line 86: done           — Task is fully completed.
Line 87: error          — Unrecoverable error (explain in reasoning).
Line 89: TEXT INPUT STRATEGY (follow this order):
Line 90: 1. BEST:  Use "fill" with a CSS selector — it clears the field and sets the value atomically.
Line 91: Example: {"action":"fill","target":"input[name='q']","text":"search query"}
Line 92: 2. GOOD:  Use "paste" — writes text via clipboard. Works when you know the field is focused.
Line 93: 3. LAST:  Use "type" — keystroke simulation. Click the field first. May fail if focus is lost.
Line 95: FORM SUBMISSION STRATEGY (follow this order):
Line 96: 1. BEST:  Use "evaluate_js" — text = "document.querySelector('form').submit()" or
Line 97: text = "document.querySelector('button[type=submit],input[type=submit]').click()"
Line 98: 2. GOOD:  Use "key" with text = "Enter" after clicking inside a form field.
Line 99: 3. LAST:  Use "click" on the submit button coordinates. If it fails twice, switch to option 1.
Line 101: RECOVERY STRATEGIES (when an action fails or doesn't work):
Line 102: - fill timeout: The CSS selector is wrong. Use evaluate_js to discover actual field names:
Line 103: {"action":"evaluate_js","text":"JSON.stringify([...document.querySelectorAll('input,textarea,select')].map(e=>({tag:e.tagName,name:e.name,id:e.id,type:e.type})))"}
Line 104: Then retry fill with the correct selector.
Line 105: - fill timeout: Alternative — click the field at its coordinates, then use "type" to enter text.
Line 106: - click not working (page unchanged after 2 attempts): Use evaluate_js to click via JavaScript:
Line 107: {"action":"evaluate_js","text":"document.querySelector('button[type=submit]').click()"}
Line 108: - click not working: Try "key" with text="Enter" while a form field is focused.
Line 109: - click not working: Try "scroll_to" first to ensure the element is fully in viewport, then click.
Line 110: - find_element fails: Use ONLY the exact visible text, not a description with extra words.
Line 112: DATA EXTRACTION & COMPLETION:
Line 113: - When you use evaluate_js or get_text, the execution result is recorded in your action history.
Line 114: You can see it in the "→ Result: ..." suffix of previous steps.
Line 115: - NEVER re-extract data you already have. Check your previous action results first.
Line 116: - If you have collected all required data, return "done" immediately with a structured summary
Line 117: in the "reasoning" field. Include the extracted data in your reasoning.
Line 118: - After extracting data from a page, move to the next subtask (next tab, close tabs, etc.).
Line 119: Do NOT call evaluate_js again on the same page — the result will be identical.
Line 120: - If the task asks for structured output (e.g. JSON), compile it from your previous results
Line 121: and include it in your "done" reasoning.
Line 123: RULES:
Line 124: 1. Analyze the screenshot carefully. Identify all UI elements, their positions, and text.
Line 125: 2. Issue exactly ONE action per turn.
Line 126: 3. Use precise coordinates from the screenshot. Viewport: {viewport_width}x{viewport_height}.
Line 127: 4. Aim for the CENTER of UI elements. Avoid clicking near edges.
Line 128: 5. After open_url, issue "wait" to let the page load.
Line 129: 6. Dismiss overlays/popups/cookie-banners before proceeding.
Line 130: 7. When the task is complete, return "done". If stuck after 2 failed attempts, try a DIFFERENT approach (see RECOVERY STRATEGIES).
Line 131: 8. If truly stuck after trying all recovery strategies, return "error" with explanation.
Line 132: 9. NEVER repeat the same failing action more than twice. Switch strategy immediately.
Line 133: 10. NEVER call evaluate_js or get_text with the same code on the same page more than once.
Line 134: The result is already in your history — use it.
Line 136: RESPONSE FORMAT — respond with ONLY this JSON:
Line 137: {
Line 138: "action": "click",
Line 139: "target": "description of element",
Line 140: "coordinates": [x, y],
Line 141: "text": "",
Line 142: "reasoning": "brief explanation"
Line 143: }
Line 144: """
Line 146: SYSTEM_PROMPT_PLAYWRIGHT_MCP = """You are a computer-using agent. You interact with a browser via Playwright MCP tools. You target elements using refs from the accessibility-tree snapshot — NOT pixel coordinates.
Line 148: Each turn you receive the current accessibility-tree snapshot as text. Element refs look like [ref=S12] in the snapshot. Use these ref values in your tool_args.
Line 150: AVAILABLE MCP TOOLS (issue exactly ONE per turn):
Line 152: NAVIGATION
Line 153: browser_navigate              — Navigate to a URL. tool_args: {"url": "https://..."}
Line 154: browser_navigate_back         — Go back in browser history. tool_args: {}
Line 156: INTERACTION
Line 157: browser_click                 — Click an element. tool_args: {"element": "description", "ref": "S12"}
Line 158: Optional: "doubleClick": true, "button": "right", "modifiers": ["Control"]
Line 159: browser_hover                 — Hover over an element. tool_args: {"element": "description", "ref": "S12"}
Line 160: browser_drag                  — Drag between elements. tool_args: {"startElement": "desc", "startRef": "S1", "endElement": "desc", "endRef": "S2"}
Line 162: INPUT
Line 163: browser_type                  — Type text into a field. tool_args: {"element": "description", "ref": "S12", "text": "value"}
Line 164: Optional: "submit": true (press Enter after), "slowly": true
Line 165: THIS IS THE MOST RELIABLE WAY TO ENTER TEXT.
Line 166: browser_press_key             — Press a key: tool_args: {"key": "Enter"} or {"key": "Control+a"}
Line 167: browser_select_option         — Select dropdown option. tool_args: {"element": "description", "ref": "S12", "values": ["option"]}
Line 168: browser_fill_form             — Fill multiple fields. tool_args: {"fields": [{"element": "...", "ref": "...", "value": "..."}]}
Line 169: browser_file_upload           — Upload files. tool_args: {"paths": ["/path/to/file"]}
Line 171: DATA EXTRACTION
Line 172: browser_snapshot              — Get the accessibility tree snapshot. tool_args: {}
Line 173: browser_evaluate              — Run JavaScript. tool_args: {"function": "() => document.title"}
Line 174: For element: {"function": "(el) => el.value", "element": "desc", "ref": "S12"}
Line 175: browser_console_messages      — Get console logs. tool_args: {} or {"level": "error"}
Line 176: browser_network_requests      — Get network activity. tool_args: {} or {"includeStatic": true}
Line 177: browser_take_screenshot       — Capture a visual screenshot. tool_args: {} or {"fullPage": true}
Line 179: EXECUTION
Line 180: browser_run_code              — Run a Playwright code snippet. tool_args: {"code": "..."}
Line 182: CONTROL
Line 183: browser_wait_for              — Wait for text/element. tool_args: {"text": "Loading"} or {"time": 3}
Line 184: browser_handle_dialog         — Handle alert/confirm. tool_args: {"accept": true} or {"accept": false, "promptText": "..."}
Line 185: browser_resize                — Resize viewport. tool_args: {"width": 1280, "height": 720}
Line 186: browser_close                 — Close the browser session. tool_args: {}
Line 187: browser_tabs                  — List/switch tabs. tool_args: {}
Line 188: browser_install               — Install a browser binary. tool_args: {}
Line 190: AGENT CONTROL (not MCP tools)
Line 191: done                          — Task completed. Summarize results in reasoning.
Line 192: error                         — Unrecoverable error. Explain in reasoning.
Line 193: wait                          — Pause 1-10 seconds. text = seconds (default 2).
Line 195: HOW TO USE tool_args (CRITICAL):
Line 196: - Set "action" to the exact MCP tool name (e.g. "browser_click", "browser_navigate").
Line 197: - Set "tool_args" to a dict matching the tool's parameter schema (see examples above).
Line 198: - For element-targeting tools (click, type, hover, drag, select_option): you MUST provide
Line 199: the "ref" value from the accessibility snapshot. Look for [ref=XXX] in the snapshot text
Line 200: and use that exact ref string.
Line 201: - "element" is a human-readable description of what you're targeting (for logging).
Line 202: - You do NOT use pixel coordinates. Always set coordinates to [0, 0].
Line 203: - LEGACY FALLBACK: If you omit tool_args and provide target/text instead, the system will
Line 204: attempt automatic ref resolution. This is less reliable — prefer tool_args.
Line 206: DATA EXTRACTION & COMPLETION:
Line 207: - When you use browser_evaluate, the result is in your action history ("→ Result: ...").
Line 208: - NEVER re-extract data you already have. Check your previous steps first.
Line 209: - Once you have all required data, return "done" with a summary in reasoning.
Line 211: RULES:
Line 212: 1. Study the accessibility-tree snapshot to find element refs [ref=XXX] before acting.
Line 213: 2. Issue exactly ONE action per turn.
Line 214: 3. Use ref values from the snapshot in tool_args — do NOT guess refs.
Line 215: 4. After browser_navigate, use "wait" to let the page load.
Line 216: 5. Use "browser_snapshot" when you need to discover what elements are available.
Line 217: 6. When done, return "done". If stuck after 3+ attempts, return "error".
Line 218: 7. NEVER repeat the same browser_evaluate call — the result is already recorded.
Line 220: RESPONSE FORMAT — respond with ONLY this JSON:
Line 221: {
Line 222: "action": "browser_click",
Line 223: "tool_args": {"element": "Submit button", "ref": "S12"},
Line 224: "coordinates": [0, 0],
Line 225: "target": "",
Line 226: "text": "",
Line 227: "reasoning": "Clicking the submit button to complete the form"
Line 228: }
Line 229: """
Line 233: def build_dynamic_mcp_prompt(discovered_tools: list[dict[str, Any]] | None = None) -> str:
Line 234: """Build the ``playwright_mcp`` system prompt from server-discovered tools.
Line 236: Pure MCP architecture: the server defines tools via ``tools/list``,
Line 237: the client discovers them, and the prompt reflects exactly what is
Line 238: available.  No static action catalog or compile-time tool knowledge.
Line 240: When *discovered_tools* is ``None`` or empty (server not yet connected),
Line 241: falls back to the static ``SYSTEM_PROMPT_PLAYWRIGHT_MCP``.
Line 243: Args:
Line 244: discovered_tools: List of tool dicts from ``get_discovered_tools()``.
Line 245: Each has ``name``, ``description``, ``inputSchema``.
Line 247: Returns:
Line 248: Complete system prompt string.
Line 249: """
Line 250: if not discovered_tools:
Line 251: return SYSTEM_PROMPT_PLAYWRIGHT_MCP
Line 253: # Build tool catalog directly from server-reported schemas
Line 254: tool_lines: list[str] = []
Line 255: for tool in sorted(discovered_tools, key=lambda t: t["name"]):
Line 256: name = tool["name"]
Line 257: desc = (tool.get("description") or "").split("\n")[0]
Line 258: schema = tool.get("inputSchema") or {}
Line 259: props = schema.get("properties") or {}
Line 260: required = set(schema.get("required") or [])
Line 262: # Format parameters compactly: name*:type for required, name:type for optional
Line 263: if props:
Line 264: params = []
Line 265: for pname, pinfo in props.items():
Line 266: ptype = pinfo.get("type", "any")
Line 267: req_mark = "*" if pname in required else ""
Line 268: params.append(f"{pname}{req_mark}:{ptype}")
Line 269: params_str = "  (" + ", ".join(params) + ")"
Line 270: else:
Line 271: params_str = ""
Line 273: tool_lines.append(f"  {name:<30} — {desc}")
Line 274: if params_str:
Line 275: tool_lines.append(f"   {params_str}")
Line 277: tools_section = "\n".join(tool_lines)
Line 279: return f"""You are a computer-using agent. You interact with a browser via Playwright MCP tools. You target elements using refs from the accessibility-tree snapshot — NOT pixel coordinates.
Line 281: Each turn you receive the current accessibility-tree snapshot as text. Element refs look like [ref=S12] in the snapshot. Use these ref values in your tool_args.
Line 283: AVAILABLE MCP TOOLS (from server — issue exactly ONE per turn):
Line 284: {tools_section}
Line 286: AGENT CONTROL (not MCP tools):
Line 287: done                           — Task completed. Summarize results in reasoning.
Line 288: error                          — Unrecoverable error. Explain in reasoning.
Line 289: wait                           — Pause 1-10 seconds. text = seconds (default 2).
Line 291: HOW TO USE tool_args (CRITICAL):
Line 292: - Set "action" to the exact tool name (e.g. "browser_click", "browser_navigate").
Line 293: - Set "tool_args" to a dict matching the tool's parameter schema shown above.
Line 294: - For element-targeting tools (click, type, hover, drag, select_option): you MUST provide
Line 295: the "ref" value from the accessibility snapshot. Look for [ref=XXX] in the snapshot text
Line 296: and use that exact ref string.
Line 297: - "element" is a human-readable description of what you're targeting (for logging).
Line 298: - You do NOT use pixel coordinates. Always set coordinates to [0, 0].
Line 299: - LEGACY FALLBACK: If you omit tool_args and provide target/text instead, the system will
Line 300: attempt automatic ref resolution. This is less reliable — prefer tool_args.
Line 302: DATA EXTRACTION & COMPLETION:
Line 303: - When you use browser_evaluate, the result is in your action history ("→ Result: ...").
Line 304: - NEVER re-extract data you already have. Check your previous steps first.
Line 305: - Once you have all required data, return "done" with a summary in reasoning.
Line 307: RULES:
Line 308: 1. Study the accessibility-tree snapshot to find element refs [ref=XXX] before acting.
Line 309: 2. Issue exactly ONE action per turn.
Line 310: 3. Use ref values from the snapshot in tool_args — do NOT guess refs.
Line 311: 4. After browser_navigate, use "wait" to let the page load.
Line 312: 5. Use "browser_snapshot" when you need to discover what elements are available.
Line 313: 6. When done, return "done". If stuck after 3+ attempts, return "error".
Line 314: 7. NEVER repeat the same browser_evaluate call — the result is already recorded.
Line 316: RESPONSE FORMAT — respond with ONLY this JSON:
Line 317: {{
Line 318: "action": "browser_click",
Line 319: "tool_args": {{"element": "Submit button", "ref": "S12"}},
Line 320: "coordinates": [0, 0],
Line 321: "target": "",
Line 322: "text": "",
Line 323: "reasoning": "Clicking the submit button to complete the form"
Line 324: }}
Line 325: """
Line 328: SYSTEM_PROMPT_XDOTOOL = """You are a computer-using agent. You see the screen via screenshots and control the full X11 desktop via xdotool. You can interact with ANY application, not just browsers.
Line 330: AVAILABLE ACTIONS (issue exactly ONE per turn):
Line 332: MOUSE / INTERACTION
Line 333: click          — Left-click at [x, y].                    coordinates required
Line 334: double_click   — Double-click at [x, y].                  coordinates required
Line 335: right_click    — Right-click at [x, y].                   coordinates required
Line 336: middle_click   — Middle-click at [x, y].                  coordinates required
Line 337: hover          — Move cursor to [x, y] without clicking.  coordinates required
Line 338: drag           — Drag from [x1,y1] to [x2,y2].           coordinates [x1,y1,x2,y2]
Line 340: INPUT (prefer paste > type for reliability)
Line 341: type           — Type text keystroke-by-keystroke. text required.
Line 342: Must click the target field first.
Line 343: key            — Press a key or combo. text required.
Line 344: xdotool keys: "Return" (not Enter), "BackSpace", "Tab",
Line 345: "Up","Down","Left","Right", "Prior"(PgUp), "Next"(PgDn),
Line 346: "ctrl+a", "shift+Tab"
Line 347: hotkey         — Multi-key combo. text = "Ctrl+Shift+T" (plus-separated).
Line 348: paste          — Paste text via clipboard + Ctrl+V. text = content.
Line 349: More reliable than type for long or special-char text.
Line 350: copy           — Copy current selection via Ctrl+C.
Line 352: NAVIGATION
Line 353: open_url       — Open a URL with xdg-open. text = full URL.
Line 355: SCROLLING
Line 356: scroll         — Scroll at [x, y]. text = "up" or "down". coordinates required.
Line 358: DESKTOP / WINDOW MANAGEMENT
Line 359: focus_window   — Bring a window to focus by name. target = window title or class.
Line 360: Example: target = "Firefox", target = "Terminal"
Line 361: open_app       — Launch an application. target = command.
Line 362: Example: target = "firefox", target = "xfce4-terminal", target = "nautilus"
Line 363: close_window   — Safely close a window via EWMH. target = window title or class.
Line 364: Example: target = "Firefox", target = "Terminal"
Line 365: This is the SAFE way to close windows. NEVER use alt+F4.
Line 367: SHELL / TERMINAL
Line 368: run_command    — Execute a shell command. text = command string.
Line 369: Returns stdout+stderr. Timeout: 30s. Desktop mode only.
Line 370: open_terminal  — Open a terminal emulator window (xfce4-terminal). No arguments needed.
Line 372: VISION
Line 373: screenshot_region — Capture a screen region. coordinates = [x, y, width, height].
Line 375: CONTROL
Line 376: wait           — Pause 1-10 seconds. text = seconds (default 2).
Line 378: TERMINAL
Line 379: done           — Task completed.
Line 380: error          — Unrecoverable error.
Line 382: NOT AVAILABLE IN XDOTOOL MODE:
Line 383: select_option (no DOM — use click to select)
Line 384: evaluate_js, get_text, find_element (no DOM / browser context)
Line 385: scroll_to (no DOM — use scroll repeatedly)
Line 387: APPROXIMATED IN XDOTOOL MODE (via keyboard shortcuts):
Line 388: fill → Ctrl+A, Delete, then type
Line 389: clear_input → Ctrl+A, Delete
Line 390: reload → F5
Line 391: go_back → Alt+Left
Line 392: go_forward → Alt+Right
Line 393: new_tab → Ctrl+T
Line 394: close_tab → Ctrl+W
Line 395: switch_tab → Ctrl+1..9 or Ctrl+PageDown
Line 396: wait_for → waits ~3 seconds
Line 398: XDOTOOL KEY NAME DIFFERENCES:
Line 399: Enter    → "Return"
Line 400: Backspace → "BackSpace"
Line 401: Page Up   → "Prior"
Line 402: Page Down → "Next"
Line 403: Arrows    → "Up", "Down", "Left", "Right"
Line 405: TEXT INPUT STRATEGY:
Line 406: 1. BEST: Use "paste" — writes via clipboard. Reliable for long text and special characters.
Line 407: 2. GOOD: Use "type" — keystroke-by-keystroke. Click the field first!
Line 408: 3. FALLBACK: Use "key" per character — sends individual keysyms. Works on Athena/Xaw widgets.
Line 410: DESKTOP CALCULATION STRATEGY:
Line 411: When performing arithmetic on a calculator app:
Line 412: 1. After opening the calculator, use focus_window to activate it.
Line 413: 2. Try paste with the expression first (e.g. paste "98765*4321/123"), then key "Return".
Line 414: 3. If paste doesn't update the display, use run_command as a CLI fallback:
Line 415: run_command with text = "echo 'scale=10; 98765*4321/123' | bc"
Line 416: or: run_command with text = "python3 -c 'print(98765*4321/123)'"
Line 417: 4. NEVER spend more than 3 attempts clicking calculator buttons — switch to run_command.
Line 419: BROWSER MODAL HANDLING:
Line 420: When opening a browser, first-run dialogs may appear (Welcome, Sign-in, Keyring).
Line 421: The system auto-dismisses most known modals, but if you see one:
Line 422: 1. Use close_window with target matching the modal title (e.g. "Welcome to Google Chrome").
Line 423: 2. If close_window fails, press key "Escape" or key "Return" to dismiss.
Line 424: 3. NEVER spend more than 2 steps on any modal — use close_window then move on.
Line 425: 4. If a "Choose password for new keyring" dialog appears, press key "Return" twice (blank password).
Line 426: 5. After dismissing modals, use wait with text "2" before continuing.
Line 428: RULES:
Line 429: 1. Analyze the screenshot carefully. Look at all visible windows, panels, buttons.
Line 430: 2. Issue exactly ONE action per turn.
Line 431: 3. Precise coordinates from the screenshot. Screen: 1440×900.
Line 432: 4. Aim for the CENTER of UI elements.
Line 433: 5. Before typing, ALWAYS click the input field first.
Line 434: 6. After open_url, issue "wait" for the application to load.
Line 435: 7. Use focus_window to switch between applications.
Line 436: 8. Use open_app to launch programs (firefox, xfce4-terminal, nautilus, etc.).
Line 437: 9. If an action fails, retry ONCE with a clearly different method (not the same click).
Line 438: 10. NEVER repeat near-identical clicks more than 2 times — IMMEDIATELY switch to keyboard/CLI.
Line 439: 11. For calculator tasks, prefer keyboard entry or run_command. See DESKTOP CALCULATION STRATEGY.
Line 440: 12. If keyboard and click both fail after one retry, use run_command as CLI fallback.
Line 441: 13. If ALL approaches fail, return "error" with exact failure reason.
Line 442: 14. When done, return "done".
Line 444: RESPONSE FORMAT — respond with ONLY this JSON:
Line 445: {
Line 446: "action": "click",
Line 447: "target": "description of element",
Line 448: "coordinates": [x, y],
Line 449: "text": "",
Line 450: "reasoning": "brief explanation"
Line 451: }
Line 452: """
Line 454: SYSTEM_PROMPT_ACCESSIBILITY = """You are a computer-using agent. You see the screen via screenshots and control the full Linux desktop via the AT-SPI accessibility tree. You target UI elements semantically by their accessible name, role, and state — NOT pixel coordinates.
Line 456: The AT-SPI engine gives you structured access to every widget in every application: buttons, text fields, menus, trees, tables, panels, and more. Physical mouse/keyboard actions are handled by xdotool under the hood.
Line 458: ENVIRONMENT:
Line 459: - The desktop is XFCE4 on Ubuntu 24.04.
Line 460: - Available applications: xfce4-settings-manager (XFCE Settings), thunar (file manager), mousepad (text editor), xfce4-terminal, firefox, google-chrome. GNOME apps (gnome-control-center) may NOT be installed.
Line 461: - To open an XFCE settings panel, use: run_command with text = "xfce4-settings-manager"
Line 462: - After launching an app, ALWAYS wait 2-3 seconds for it to start, then use get_accessibility_tree to discover its elements.
Line 464: AVAILABLE ACTIONS (issue exactly ONE per turn):
Line 466: INTERACTION (target = accessible name or role:name)
Line 467: click          — Click an element. target = accessible name or "role:name".
Line 468: AT-SPI action interface is tried first; falls back to xdotool click at center.
Line 469: double_click   — Double-click an element. target required.
Line 470: right_click    — Right-click an element. target required.
Line 471: hover          — Hover over an element. target required.
Line 473: INPUT (target = element to interact with)
Line 474: fill           — Clear + type into an element atomically.
Line 475: target = element name, text = value.
Line 476: THIS IS THE MOST RELIABLE WAY TO ENTER TEXT.
Line 477: type           — Click to focus, then keystroke-type into an element.
Line 478: target = element name, text = content.
Line 479: key            — Press a key or combo: "Return", "Tab", "ctrl+a", "BackSpace".
Line 480: text required.
Line 481: hotkey         — Multi-key combo: text = "Ctrl+Shift+T".
Line 482: clear_input    — Clear a field. target = element name.
Line 483: select_option  — Select a dropdown option by clicking. target = option name.
Line 484: paste          — Paste text via clipboard. text = content to paste.
Line 485: copy           — Copy current selection via Ctrl+C.
Line 487: NAVIGATION
Line 488: open_url       — Open URL via xdg-open. text = full URL.
Line 490: SCROLLING
Line 491: scroll         — Scroll page. text = "up" or "down".
Line 492: scroll_to      — Scroll an element into view. target = element name.
Line 494: ACCESSIBILITY TREE (semantic discovery)
Line 495: get_accessibility_tree — Dump the AT-SPI tree for an application.
Line 496: target = app name (optional). Returns element IDs, roles, names, states.
Line 497: USE THIS to discover what elements are available.
Line 498: find_element   — Find elements in the tree. Same as get_accessibility_tree.
Line 500: DESKTOP / WINDOW MANAGEMENT
Line 501: focus_window   — Activate a window by title. target = window title.
Line 502: open_terminal  — Open a terminal window (xfce4-terminal).
Line 503: run_command    — Execute a shell command. text = command string.
Line 504: Allowed: xfce4-settings-manager, xfce4-taskmanager, thunar,
Line 505: mousepad, firefox, google-chrome, gnome-calculator, and system utilities.
Line 507: CONTROL
Line 508: wait           — Pause 1-10 seconds. text = seconds (default 2).
Line 509: wait_for       — Poll for element to appear. target = element name.
Line 511: TERMINAL
Line 512: done           — Task completed. text = summary of what was accomplished.
Line 513: error          — Unrecoverable error (explain in reasoning).
Line 515: DATA EXTRACTION & COMPLETION:
Line 516: - The result of every action is appended to your history as "→ Result: ...".
Line 517: - Once you have collected the data the task asked for, IMMEDIATELY return done.
Line 518: - NEVER call get_accessibility_tree on the same application more than twice unless the UI changed.
Line 519: - NEVER repeat the same action with the same target if it already succeeded.
Line 520: - If you already have the information requested, return done with a summary — do NOT keep exploring.
Line 522: ELEMENT TARGETING STRATEGY:
Line 523: 1. Use "get_accessibility_tree" (target = app name) to see available elements with their roles and names.
Line 524: 2. Target elements by their ACCESSIBLE NAME: e.g. target = "Search", target = "Open", target = "Username".
Line 525: 3. For ambiguous names, use "role:name" syntax: target = "push button:OK", target = "text:Username".
Line 526: 4. Numeric element_id from the tree dump can also be used: target = "42".
Line 527: 5. Names are matched case-insensitively as substrings.
Line 529: APPLICATION LAUNCH STRATEGY:
Line 530: 1. Use "run_command" with text = app executable name (e.g. "xfce4-settings-manager").
Line 531: 2. Issue "wait" with text = "3" to let the application start.
Line 532: 3. Use "get_accessibility_tree" to discover the app's UI structure.
Line 533: 4. If the app failed to start (check screenshot), try an alternative app or return error.
Line 535: NOT AVAILABLE IN ACCESSIBILITY MODE:
Line 536: evaluate_js, get_html, query_selector (no DOM/browser context)
Line 537: set_cookies, upload_file, new_context (browser-only)
Line 538: screenshot_region (use screenshot instead)
Line 540: RULES:
Line 541: 1. Analyze the screenshot for visual context, then use AT-SPI tree for semantic interaction.
Line 542: 2. Issue exactly ONE action per turn.
Line 543: 3. Use "get_accessibility_tree" when you need to discover available UI elements.
Line 544: 4. Prefer semantic targeting (name/role) over coordinates — set coordinates to [0, 0].
Line 545: 5. After run_command to launch an app, issue "wait" to let the application load.
Line 546: 6. Use focus_window to switch between application windows.
Line 547: 7. When done, return "done" with a text summary. If stuck after 3+ attempts, return "error".
Line 548: 8. NEVER use gnome-control-center as the primary settings app — use xfce4-settings-manager instead.
Line 549: 9. If an action fails, check the error message. Do NOT blindly retry the same action.
Line 550: 10. NEVER call get_accessibility_tree with the same target more than twice in a row.
Line 552: RESPONSE FORMAT — respond with ONLY this JSON:
Line 553: {
Line 554: "action": "click",
Line 555: "target": "accessible element name",
Line 556: "coordinates": [0, 0],
Line 557: "text": "",
Line 558: "reasoning": "brief explanation"
Line 559: }
Line 560: """
Line 563: # ── Computer Use (native CU tool protocol) ───────────────────────────────────
Line 564: # Minimal system instruction; the model's built-in CU tool handles action
Line 565: # schema.  This prompt only provides high-level guidance.
Line 566: SYSTEM_PROMPT_COMPUTER_USE = """You are a computer-using agent that completes tasks by interacting with the screen.
Line 568: You have native computer_use capabilities. The system will convert your tool calls
Line 569: into real UI interactions (mouse clicks, keyboard input, scrolling, navigation).
Line 571: ENVIRONMENT:
Line 572: - Screen resolution: {viewport_width}x{viewport_height} (browser) or 1440x900 (desktop).
Line 573: - Browser: Chromium via Playwright (browser mode) or any X11 application (desktop mode).
Line 574: - Screenshots are captured after each action and sent back to you automatically.
Line 576: INTERACTION RULES:
Line 577: 1. Use your built-in computer_use tool for all UI interactions — do NOT describe
Line 578: actions in text; emit tool calls.
Line 579: 2. Analyse each screenshot carefully before acting. Identify exact positions of
Line 580: buttons, links, text fields, and other interactive elements.
Line 581: 3. Click precisely at the CENTER of UI elements — avoid edges.
Line 582: 4. For text entry: click the input field first (click_at), then type (type_text_at).
Line 583: By default type_text_at clears the field and presses Enter; set press_enter=false
Line 584: or clear_before_typing=false to override.
Line 585: 5. Scroll to find content not yet visible (scroll_document or scroll_at).
Line 586: 6. Use key_combination for keyboard shortcuts (e.g., "Enter", "Control+C", "Tab").
Line 587: 7. Use navigate to go to a specific URL directly.
Line 588: 8. Use go_back / go_forward for browser history navigation.
Line 589: 9. Use wait_5_seconds when a page or application needs time to load.
Line 591: COMPLETION:
Line 592: - When the task is complete, state the result clearly in your final text response.
Line 593: Do NOT emit a tool call in your final turn.
Line 594: - If you are stuck after 3 attempts at the same action, explain the blocker in text.
Line 596: SAFETY:
Line 597: - Some actions may include a safety_decision requiring confirmation. Follow the
Line 598: system's guidance.
Line 599: - Do NOT interact with CAPTCHAs or security challenges unless you receive explicit
Line 600: user confirmation.
Line 601: - Do NOT enter passwords, credit card numbers, or other sensitive data unless the
Line 602: task explicitly requires it and you have user confirmation.
Line 604: IMPORTANT:
Line 605: - You see the FULL screen (browser viewport or desktop).
Line 606: - All coordinates in your tool calls are automatically mapped to the screen.
Line 607: - Gemini: coordinates are normalized (0-999 grid) — the system handles scaling.
Line 608: - Claude: coordinates are real pixel values.
Line 609: """
Line 612: def get_system_prompt(
Line 613: engine: str,
Line 614: mode: str = "browser",
Line 615: discovered_tools: list[dict[str, Any]] | None = None,
Line 616: ) -> str:
Line 617: """Return the system prompt for a given engine.
Line 619: For ``playwright_mcp``, when *discovered_tools* is provided the prompt
Line 620: is **built dynamically** from the tools the MCP server reported via
Line 621: ``tools/list``.  This ensures the prompt always matches what the server
Line 622: actually offers — no hardcoded tool lists that can drift.
Line 624: Falls back to mode-based selection for backward compatibility.
Line 625: Dynamically injects actual viewport dimensions for the Playwright engine.
Line 626: """
Line 627: from backend.config import config
Line 629: # Actual viewport dimensions (must match agent_service.py browser init)
Line 630: vw = str(config.screen_width - 100)
Line 631: vh = str(config.screen_height - 80)
Line 633: def _inject_viewport(prompt: str) -> str:
Line 634: return prompt.replace("{viewport_width}", vw).replace("{viewport_height}", vh)
Line 636: # Playwright MCP: build from discovered tools when available
Line 637: if engine == "playwright_mcp":
Line 638: return build_dynamic_mcp_prompt(discovered_tools)
Line 640: prompts = {
Line 641: "omni_accessibility": SYSTEM_PROMPT_ACCESSIBILITY,
Line 642: "computer_use": _inject_viewport(SYSTEM_PROMPT_COMPUTER_USE),
Line 643: }
Line 645: if engine in prompts:
Line 646: return prompts[engine]
Line 648: # Fallback: derive from mode
Line 649: if mode == "desktop":
Line 650: return SYSTEM_PROMPT_ACCESSIBILITY
Line 651: return build_dynamic_mcp_prompt(discovered_tools)
Line 654: # ── Prompt / Schema drift detection ──────────────────────────────────────────
Line 656: # Maps engine name → (prompt_string, display_label)
Line 657: # NOTE: playwright_mcp is excluded — its prompt is built dynamically from
Line 658: # server-discovered tools, so static drift detection does not apply.
Line 659: _ENGINE_PROMPT_MAP: dict[str, tuple[str, str]] = {
Line 660: "omni_accessibility": (SYSTEM_PROMPT_ACCESSIBILITY, "Omni Accessibility"),
Line 661: "computer_use": (SYSTEM_PROMPT_COMPUTER_USE, "Computer Use"),
Line 662: }
Line 664: # Regex that captures bare action names from prompt text
Line 665: # Matches lines like: "  click          — Left-click at ..."
Line 666: _ACTION_LINE_RE = re.compile(
Line 667: r"^\s{1,4}(\w+)\s+—", re.MULTILINE
Line 668: )
Line 671: def _extract_prompt_actions(prompt_text: str) -> set[str]:
Line 672: """Extract action keywords from a system prompt string."""
Line 673: return {m.group(1) for m in _ACTION_LINE_RE.finditer(prompt_text)}
Line 676: def validate_prompt_actions() -> list[str]:
Line 677: """Cross-check actions mentioned in prompts against the capability schema.
Line 679: Returns a list of human-readable warning strings.  An empty list means
Line 680: full alignment.  Called at server startup to surface drift early.
Line 681: """
Line 682: from backend.engine_capabilities import EngineCapabilities
Line 684: caps = EngineCapabilities()
Line 685: warnings: list[str] = []
Line 687: for engine_name, (prompt_text, label) in _ENGINE_PROMPT_MAP.items():
Line 688: prompt_actions = _extract_prompt_actions(prompt_text)
Line 689: if not prompt_actions:
Line 690: # Prompt format may differ; skip if nothing was extracted
Line 691: continue
Line 693: schema_actions = caps.get_engine_actions(engine_name)
Line 695: # Actions in prompt but not in schema → schema may need updating
Line 696: extra = prompt_actions - schema_actions
Line 697: if extra:
Line 698: msg = (
Line 699: f"[{label}] Prompt mentions actions not in engine_capabilities.json: "
Line 700: f"{sorted(extra)}"
Line 701: )
Line 702: warnings.append(msg)
Line 703: logger.warning(msg)
Line 705: # Actions in schema but not in prompt is expected (prompts are curated
Line 706: # subsets), so we only log at DEBUG for awareness.
Line 707: missing = schema_actions - prompt_actions - {"done", "error",
Line 708: "focus_and_type", "safe_type", "retry_click", "verify_input",
Line 709: "paste_fallback"}
Line 710: if missing:
Line 711: logger.debug(
Line 712: "[%s] Schema actions not in prompt (OK, prompts are curated): %s",
Line 713: label, sorted(missing),
Line 714: )
Line 716: return warnings


=== FILE: backend/agent/screenshot.py ===
--- SOURCE CODE ---
"""Screenshot capture via the internal agent service.

Calls the HTTP API exposed by the agent service running inside the
container. Supports 'browser' (Playwright) and 'desktop' (scrot) modes.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import asyncio
import base64
import logging

import httpx

from backend.config import config

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# Reusable async client
_http_client: httpx.AsyncClient | None = None


def _get_client() -> httpx.AsyncClient:
    """Return or create the module-level reusable httpx client."""
    global _http_client
    if _http_client is None or _http_client.is_closed:
        _http_client = httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=15.0)
    return _http_client


async def capture_screenshot(mode: str = "browser", engine: str | None = None) -> str:
    """Capture a PNG screenshot and return base64 string.

    When *engine* is ``playwright_mcp``, captures the screenshot from the
    MCP-controlled browser (which is a separate headless instance) so the
    model sees what MCP actually controls.  For all other engines the
    screenshot comes from the in-container agent service as before.

    Args:
        mode: 'browser' or 'desktop'.
        engine: Optional engine name — 'playwright_mcp' triggers MCP route.

    Returns:
        Base64-encoded PNG string.
    """
    # NOTE: playwright_mcp engine uses accessibility-tree snapshots instead
    # of screenshots.  The snapshot is captured directly in the agent loop
    # (loop.py) and sent as text to the model — no image needed.

    # ── Default: screenshot via agent_service ─────────────────────────────
    url = f"{config.agent_service_url}/screenshot?mode={mode}"
    client = _get_client()

    try:
        resp = await client.get(url)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()

        if "error" in data:
            raise RuntimeError(data["error"])

        b64 = data["screenshot"]
        method = data.get("method", "unknown")
        logger.debug("Screenshot captured via %s (%d chars)", method, len(b64))
        return b64

    except (httpx.ConnectError, httpx.TimeoutException) as e:
        logger.warning("Agent service unreachable, falling back to docker exec: %s", e)
        return await _fallback_docker_screenshot()


async def _fallback_docker_screenshot() -> str:
    """Fallback: grab screenshot via docker exec + scrot."""
    name = config.container_name

    proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
        "docker", "exec", name, "scrot", "-z", "-o", "/tmp/screenshot.png",
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE, stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
    )
    _, stderr = await proc.communicate()

    if proc.returncode != 0:
        proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
            "docker", "exec", name, "import", "-window", "root", "/tmp/screenshot.png",
            stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE, stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
        )
        _, stderr = await proc.communicate()
        if proc.returncode != 0:
            raise RuntimeError(f"Screenshot capture failed: {stderr.decode().strip()}")

    proc_read = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
        "docker", "exec", name, "cat", "/tmp/screenshot.png",
        stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE, stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
    )
    stdout, stderr = await proc_read.communicate()

    if proc_read.returncode != 0 or not stdout:
        raise RuntimeError(f"Failed to read screenshot: {stderr.decode().strip()}")

    b64 = base64.b64encode(stdout).decode("ascii")
    logger.info("Screenshot via fallback: %d bytes", len(stdout))
    return b64


async def get_screenshot_bytes() -> bytes:
    """Return raw PNG bytes of the current screen."""
    b64 = await capture_screenshot()
    return base64.b64decode(b64)


async def check_service_health() -> bool:
    """Check if the internal agent service is responsive."""
    url = f"{config.agent_service_url}/health"
    client = _get_client()
    try:
        resp = await client.get(url, timeout=3.0)
        return resp.status_code == 200
    except Exception:
        return False
